title: Ethereum's Upgrade Broke Everything
transcript: Let's  face  it,  the  cryptobull  market   sucked.  While  Bitcoin  enjoyed  its  time   in  the  sun,  altcoins  barely  moved,  which   was  disappointing  to  say  the  least.  Take   Ethereum's  ETH  for  example.  Despite   being  the  second  largest  crypto  by   market  cap,  it  barely  peaked  above  the   all-time  high  from  the  2021  cycle.  And   now  that  we're  in  a  bare  market,   everyone  is  wondering  how  low  ETH  could   go  and  what  challenges  it  could  face   going  forward.  So,  when  a  major  research   company  released  a  report  announcing   they  were  bearish  on  ETH  and  were  going   short  on  Bitine,  the  biggest  Ethereum   treasury  company,  we  knew  we  had  to  tell   you  all  about  it.  Now,  that  report  is   titled  quote  Ethereum  ETHUSD,  what   Vitalik  knows  and  Tom  Lee  doesn't.  It   was  published  by  Kulpa  Research,  which   is  known  for  its  investigative  deep   dives.  As  always,  we'll  be  giving  you   the  juiciest  details  right  here  in  this   video,  but  we'll  leave  a  link  to  the   full  report  in  the  description  for  your   convenience  and  reading  pleasure.  Now,   the  report  starts  by  saying  that  today   Ethereum  has  two  core  use  cases,  serving   as  the  settlement  layer  for  stable  coins   and  hosting  tokenized  realworld  assets   or  RWA.  The  report's  authors  explained   that  Ethereum  is  known  for  being  slow   and  expensive,  especially  compared  to   competing  chains  like  Salana.  However,   Ethereum's  Fusaka  upgrade,  which  was   implemented  in  December  2025,  increased   the  network's  gas  limit  from  30  million   units  to  60  million  units  in  an  effort   to  enhance  scalability.  Keep  this  in   mind  for  later.  Anyway,  the  authors   highlight  the  number  of  Ethereum   transactions  which  have  skyrocketed   since  Fusaka.  This  typically  doesn't   happen  when  other  major  cryptos  like  for   instance  BNB  or  Bitcoin  have  major   upgrades  of  their  own.  As  such,  many  see   this  as  a  sign  that  Fusaka  was  hugely   successful.  In  fact,  Vitalic  Buterin   even  tweeted  in  January  that  Fusarka  had   solved  Ethereum's  trillemma  problem,   meaning  it  can  now  achieve   decentralization,  security,  and   scalability  all  at  once.  And  by  the  way,   you  can  learn  all  about  Fusaka  in  more   detail  in  the  video  right  over  here.   Anyhow,  nobody  has  been  more  bullish  on   Ethereum's  post  Fusaka  future  than   Bitmines's  chairman,  Tom  Lee.  Now,  for   those  unaware,  Bitmine  is  the  world's   largest  Ethereum  treasury  company.  You   can  think  of  it  as  being  Ethereum's  own   version  of  Micro  Strategy  and  Tom  Lee  as   Ethereum's  own  Michael  Sailor.  Case  in   point,  Lee  once  predicted  ETH  could   reach  as  high  as  $15,000  by  well  the  end   of  2025  and  revised  his  target  in   December  to  suggest  that  ETH  could  hit   as  high  as  $9,000  by  uh  January  this   year.  H  if  only.  In  any  case,  Lee  argues   that  the  surge  in  active  addresses  and   transaction  count  shows  Ethereum   strength.  He  even  said  that  quote   Ethereum  is  not  in  a  death  spiral   because  utility  is  going  up,  adding  that   this  reflects  growing  institutional   adoption.  But  this  report  disagrees.  Its   authors  argue  that  Lee  is  either   ignorant  or  too  blindsided  to  notice   that  the  bullish  metrics  he  cites  are   actually  driven  by  bearish  factors.  And   who  do  the  authors  turn  to  to  back  up   their  bearish  thesis?  Why  none  other   than  Vitalic  Butarium  himself,  who  has   recently  been  selling  a  boatload  of  ETH.   Vitalik  announced  in  late  January  this   year  that  he  would  sell  over  16,000  ETH,   declaring  that  quote,  "The  Ethereum   Foundation  is  entering  a  period  of  mild   austerity.  The  following  month,  he   dumped  over  19,000  ETH."  And  even  at   today's  prices,  that's  tens  of  millions   of  dollars  worth.  Now,  in  fairness,  the   austerity  Vitalik  mentioned  had  two   goals.  Speed  up  Ethereum's  development   to  stay  on  track  with  its  roadmap  and   sustain  the  Ethereum  Foundation   long-term  to  ensure  that  development   actually  happens.  And  in  our  view,  if   ever  there's  a  good  time  to  focus  on   development,  it's  during  the  bare  market   we  now  find  ourselves  in.  Regardless,   the  report's  authors  argue  that   Ethereum's  apparent  surge  in  activity   isn't  as  positive  as  it  seems.  They  even   speculate  that  Vitalik's  recent  selling   could  signal  that  Fusaka  may  have  broken   ETH's  tokconomics,  something  that   permable  Tom  Lee  seemingly  hasn't   grasped.  More  on  that  later,  though.  And   by  the  way,  if  you're  enjoying  the  video   so  far,  then  smash  that  like  button.  And   if  you  want  to  stay  on  top  of  the  latest   Ethereum  news,  whether  it's  a  major   upgrade  or  Vitalik  selling  more  ETH,   then  you  can  get  our  weekly  newsletter   sent  straight  to  your  inbox.  That's   where  our  research  team  breaks  down  the   biggest  stories  shaping  the  crypto   market  and  highlights  key  catalysts  for   the  week  ahead.  And  best  of  all,  it's   completely  free.  So  why  not  sign  up  now   using  the  link  below  or  by  scanning  the   QR  code  on  screen  now.  Okay,  so  the  next   part  of  the  report  explains  that  the   authors  analyzed  onchain  activity  on   Ethereum  along  with  several  major  stable   coins  running  on  the  network  like  USDT   and  USDC.  Their  goal  was  to  identify   poisoning  transactions,  noting  this  may   be  the  first  time  such  analysis  has  been   done,  which  is  pretty  wild.  But  what   exactly  are  poisoning  transactions?   Well,  in  a  nutshell,  address  poisoning   is  a  scam  where  attackers  send  victims   tiny  so-called  dust  transactions  from  a   wallet  address  that  closely  resembles   the  one  they  previously  interacted  with.   This  places  the  fraudulent  address  in   the  victim's  transaction  history,  which   can  lead  users  to  mistakenly  copy  it  for   use  in  future  transfers.  Now,  on  the   surface,  the  odds  of  someone  falling  for   this  can  seem  extremely  low.  But  because   Dust  transactions  are  extremely  cheap  to   send,  attackers  can  target  millions  of   crypto  wallets  and  make  a  profit  if  even   a  small  fraction  of  victims  send  funds   to  these  addresses.  Now,  this  is  already   pretty  scary,  but  it  gets  worse.  The   reports  authors  explained  that  before   the  Fusaka  upgrade,  attackers  focused  on   alternative  blockchains  like  BNB  Smart   Chain,  where  transaction  costs  are  much   lower.  Specifically,  between  2022  and   2024,  there  were  252  million  poison   transactions  on  BNB  compared  to  just  17   million  on  Ethereum.  simply  because  BNB   transactions  cost  only  about  a  cent.  But   since  the  Fusaka  upgrade  made  Ethereum   transactions  much  cheaper,  these  attacks   have  become  more  viable.  That's  because   the  Fusaka  upgrade  lowered  transaction   fees  by  about  90%.   Now,  a  poisoning  attempt  costs  roughly  3   cents  per  address,  higher  than  BNB,  but   still  much  lower  than  before.   Considering  the  size  of  the  Ethereum   ecosystem  compared  to  BNBs,  well,  it's   easy  to  see  how  lucrative  these  attacks   could  become  if  attackers  focused  on   Ethereum  wallets  instead.  And  if  you're   thinking,  "Sure,  it's  hypothetically   possible,  but  it  probably  won't  matter,"   think  again.  According  to  the  authors,   quote,  "Our  analysis  found  that  since   Fusaka  across  both  ETH  and  major  ETH   stable  coins,  95%  of  the  increase  in   active  wallets  were  those  created   explicitly  for  dusting  or  address   poisoning  attacks.  Moreover,  dust   transactions  now  make  up  an  estimated   18%  of  all  Ethereum  transactions,  and   that  number  is  rising.  In  November  last   year,  before  Fusarka,  there  were  just   4.4  million  poison  transactions  or  9.5%   of  all  transactions.  By  December,  this   rose  to  6.3  million  or  12%  of  the  total.   But  in  January,  after  Fusark's   deployment,  poison  transactions  jumped   to  13  million,  over  18%  of  all  Ethereum   transactions.  And  by  February,  they  rose   again  to  nearly  14  million,  more  than   22%  of  the  total.  Now,  the  next  part  of   the  report  takes  a  closer  look  at  the   direct  impact  Fusaka  has  had  on  Ethereum   gas  fees,  starting  with  a  pretty  wild   accusation.  The  authors  explain  that   Vitalik's  gigabrain  may  have   miscalculated  Fusarka's  impact  on  gas   fees.  The  report  points  to  an  initiative   called  Pump  the  Gas  or  PTG,  a  collective   whose  focus  is  to  quote  educate  the   Ethereum  community  about  the  gas  limit   and  how  it  can  be  used  to  help  improve   Ethereum  scaling.  PTG  was  a  prominent   voice  for  a  gas  limit  increase,  gaining   the  support  of  validators  and  even  the   Ethereum  Foundation.  The  idea  was   simple.  By  increasing  the  gas  limit  to   60  million,  this  would  theoretically   bring  transaction  costs  down  by  anywhere   between  10  and  30%.  And  as  the  authors   explain,  this  theory  was  based  on  a   formula  that  Vitalik  himself  came  up   with  back  in  2018.  We'll  skip  the  boring   details,  but  essentially  Vitalik   estimated  that  increasing  the  gas  limit   would  reduce  fees  by  13  to  21%  in  line   with  PTG's  estimates.  Instead,  fees   didn't  fall  10%,  21%,  or  even  30%.  No,   they  collapsed  by  over  90%.  Needless  to   say,  that  is  not  a  small  difference.  So,   how  did  this  happen?  Well,  the  authors   say  the  oversight  comes  down  to  two  key   factors.  The  first  reason  relates  to  an   Ethereum  improvement  proposal  from  2021,   specifically  EIP1559.   Basically,  EIP1559   changed  how  gas  fees  are  structured  and   how  validators  are  rewarded.  The  goal   was  to  reduce  congestion  by  increasing   the  block  size  and  introducing  a  base   transaction  fee.  However,  the  authors   note  that  quote  the  elasticity  estimates   appear  to  have  never  been  updated  in   turn.  The  second  reason  is  that  the   Ethereum  ecosystem  is  very  different   from  how  it  was  in  2018  when  Vitalik   developed  that  formula  we  mentioned   earlier.  Today,  Ethereum  is  largely   driven  by  layer  2  solutions  like   arbitum,  optimism,  and  base,  many  of   which  didn't  exist  until  years  later.   Naturally,  Vitalik's  original   calculations  didn't  factor  these  in,  and   it  appears  PTG  didn't  either.  Whatever   the  case,  the  report's  authors  then  show   how  serious  the  dust  transaction  problem   is  with  firstirhand  evidence.  They   created  two  new  wallet  addresses  and   sent  $500  from  one  to  the  other.  In  just   5  minutes,  both  were  hit  by  poisoning   attempts  with  transactions  from  three   separate  wallets  sharing  the  same  first   and  last  characters  as  the  new   addresses.  Now,  this  is  insane  when  you   consider  that  wallet  addresses  are   randomly  generated  and  the  attacking   wallets  had  no  prior  transaction   history.  In  other  words,  an  attacker   likely  using  a  bot  to  scan  for  victims,   identified  an  Ethereum  transaction,   generated  new  wallet  addresses  until  one   closely  matched  the  target,  and  sent  a   scam  transaction  all  within  minutes.  And   if  that  wasn't  crazy  enough,  the  report   cites  research  from  Cariegi  Melon   University,  which  found  that  more  than   270  million  poisoning  attacks  occurred   between  July  2022  and  June  2024  across   both  Ethereum  and  BNB  Smart  Chain.  Over   6,600   transfers  were  made  to  these  fake   addresses,  earning  attackers  more  than   $83  million  in  illicit  gains.  But  in   just  2  months  between  December  2025  and   January  2026,  Cariegi  Melon  found  that   over  $50  million  had  been  stolen  in  the   same  way.  And  these  attacks  aren't  going   away.  At  the  time  of  the  report  being   published,  the  authors  estimated  that  at   least  $348  million  in  crypto  had  been   siphoned  through  poison  address  attacks.   It's  scary  stuff,  so  stay  vigilant  out   there.  Now,  the  final  part  of  the  report   argues  that  Fusaka  didn't  just  open  the   door  to  the  problems  we've  discussed,  it   may  have  also  broken  ETH's  tokconomics   entirely.  The  authors  explained  that   because  transaction  fees  have  plunged  on   Ethereum,  the  fee  burn  mechanism  has   also  weakened  significantly.  Now,  this   is  a  big  deal  because  as  more  blocks   fill  with  tiny  dust  transactions,   essentially  spam,  larger  legitimate   transactions  can  enter  blocks  more   easily.  This  means  they  no  longer  have   to  compete  by  paying  higher  tips  to  get   prioritized.  Now,  on  the  surface,  that   might  sound  like  good  news  for  Ethereum   users,  but  there's  a  major  trade-off.   With  fewer  tips  being  paid,  validators   are  earning  significantly  less.  This  in   turn  drives  ETH  staking  yields  down,   reducing  the  incentive  to  lock  up  ETH   and  secure  the  network  and  giving   validators  less  reason  to  keep  providing   that  security.  This  not  only  threatens   Ethereum's  security,  but  could  also   hinder  its  institutional  adoption.   That's  simply  because  institutions   prioritize  security  above  all  else,   especially  for  things  like  tokenization.   And  as  we  noted  earlier,  stable  coins   and  RWAS  are  arguably  the  biggest   drivers  of  Ethereum's  institutional   appeal.  This  is  a  serious  issue  that's   been  debated  for  years,  and  that  debate   continues  to  this  day.  In  fact,  the   authors  claimed  that  an  institutional   crypto  adviser  told  them,  quote,  "If   Blockspace  expands  while  demand  remains   stagnant,  then  the  lower  priority  fees   compress  economics  for  validators.  And   if  you  increase  capacity  without   increasing  demand,  you  get  lower  or  less   deflation  or  even  modest  inflation.  The   report  also  emphasizes  that  even  when   Ethereum  sees  high  activity,  transaction   fees  no  longer  spike  the  way  they  did   before  Fusaka.  Validators  now  earn  less   and  less  ETH  is  now  burned  through  these   transactions.  So  all  of  this  points  to   one  thing.  Ethereum's  tokconomics  are   now  much  weaker  as  a  result  of  Fusaka.   That  said,  some  argue  this  may  only  be  a   temporary  problem.  For  example,  in   January  this  year,  Gorgi  Vitzky,  the   founder  of  DeFi  protocol  Taio,  said  that   quote,  "It's  basic  supply  and  demand.   Fees  rise  when  many  transactions  compete   for  limited  block  space,  and  they  fall   when  demand  drops.  Right  now,  we're   simply  seeing  fewer  transactions."   However,  the  reports  authors  call  this   statement  insane  because  transaction   counts  on  Ethereum  are  at  an  all-time   high.  Another  example  comes  from  Leon   Vidman,  the  head  of  research  at  Lisk,  a   platform  developed  to  help  developers   build  web  3  apps.  According  to  him,   quote,  "What's  different  is  that  2021   was  a  speculationdriven  fee  explosion.   Now  it's  actual  usage  at  scale.  people   are  paying  for  real  economic  activity.   Again  though,  the  reports  authors  call   Videman  equally  clueless,  saying  that   what  we're  now  seeing  has  nothing  to  do   with  real  economic  activity,  but  a   direct  result  of  dust  transactions   enabled  by  Fusaka.  They  also  note  that   Ethereum's  daily  active  validate  account   peaked  in  July  last  year.  Moreover,   while  staking  demand  appears  to  be  near   all-time  highs,  most  of  that  apparently   comes  from  Bitine.  In  other  words,  this   demand  is  artificially  inflated  by  a   single  entity.  Without  Bit,  ETH  staking   demand  would  likely  be  only  a  fraction   of  today's  levels.  At  the  time  of  the   report's  publishing,  the  staking  queue   sat  at  over  3.3  million  ETH,  largely   driven  by  the  likes  of  Bitmine  and  other   Ethereum  Treasury  companies  who  use   staking  to  expand  their  reserves.  These   companies  argue  that  ETH  is  deflationary   and  that  staking  offers  a  better  return   on  investment  than  other  assets.   However,  the  report  notes  that  neither   claim  holds  up.  While  ETH  staking  offers   about  a  2.7%  annual  yield,  US   treasuries,  widely  considered  the  safest   investment,  yield  around  4.1%  per  year.   On  top  of  that,  ETH  is  slightly   inflationary  at  0.8%.   Now,  given  that  ETH's  value  has  dropped   while  treasuries  remain  stable,  Bitine   clearly  would  have  been  better  off   playing  it  safe.  As  such,  the  authors   make  a  number  of  damning  predictions   about  Ethereum's  future.  They  note  that   ETH's  inflation  rate  will  likely  climb   higher  than  the  current  0.8%  and  that   Ethereum  will  continue  to  lose  mind   share  to  competing  chains  like  Salana   and  could  even  be  cannibalized  by  its   own  layer  2  ecosystem.  They  note  that   this  deterioration  is  already  happening   before  our  very  eyes  and  state  that   quote  in  the  end  ETH  holders  will  be   left  with  little  of  economic  substance.   We  are  short  and  think  ETH  is  headed   lower.   And  with  that,  we've  reached  the  end  of   the  report.  And  remember,  you  can  find  a   link  to  the  full  version  in  the   description  below.  But  right  now,  you're   probably  all  wondering  what  this  means   for  Ethereum's  future.  Well,  while   Fusaka  seems  to  have  done  more  harm  than   good,  unfortunately,  it  doesn't  end   there.  That's  because  there's  also  the   upcoming  Glamsterdam  upgrade,  which  is   currently  scheduled  for  the  first  half   of  2026.  While  Fusaka  was  focused  on   improving  data  availability  for  rollups,   Glamsterdam  will  instead  focus  on   censorship  resistance  and  improving  the   efficiency  of  block  building  and   transaction  execution.  But  just  like   Fusaka,  the  broader  goal  of  Glamsterdam   is  to  enhance  Ethereum  scalability.  Now   that's  obviously  a  good  thing.  But   Glamsterdam  will  also  raise  the  gas   limit  from  60  million  to  a  whopping  200   million  units.  Naturally,  this  could   push  staking  yields  even  lower  and  could   exacerbate  the  poison  transaction   problem  we've  already  discussed.  So,  one   last  time,  folks,  stay  vigilant.   Transaction  poisoning  will  likely  keep   growing  until  a  fix  is  implemented,  if   ever.  In  the  meantime,  double  check  that   every  character  in  a  wallet  address   matches  the  one  you  intend  to  interact   with.  You  can  always  verify  addresses  on   sites  like  Etherscan  to  be  extra   certain.  Don't  take  your  eye  off  the   ball  or  you  could  accidentally  send  your   crypto  fortune  to  some  scumbag.  Now,  the   good  news  is  that  it's  not  all  doom  and   gloom.  Despite  all  of  its  flaws,   Ethereum  is  still  the  largest  crypto   behind  Bitcoin.  And  this  isn't  likely  to   change  anytime  soon.  That's  simply   because  the  majority  of  crypto  activity   still  runs  on  Ethereum.  In  DeFi,  stable   coin  transactions,  and  of  course,  RWA   tokenization.  In  case  you  didn't  know,   around  57%  of  all  tokenized  RWAs  live  on   Ethereum.  Meanwhile,  institutions  prefer   Ethereum  because  it's  provably  more   robust  and  secure  and  has  never  suffered   a  major  outage  unlike  competing  chains.   Looking  at  you,  Salana.  So,  taken   together,  this  suggests  that  ETH's  price   will  eventually  appreciate,  current   market  conditions  notwithstanding.  That   said,  the  current  bare  market  could  be  a   long-term  positive  for  Ethereum.  That's   because  historically  these  periods  are   when  developers  really  knuckle  down  and   focus  on  building  innovative  solutions.   The  next  killer  app  could  emerge  in  the   coming  years  introducing  new  tech  or   solving  existing  pain  points  and   Ethereum  is  the  logical  platform  given   its  outsiz  market  share.  So  yes,   admittedly  things  aren't  looking  great   right  now,  but  we  reckon  this  pain  is   temporary.  If  Ethereum's  developers  have   shown  us  anything  over  the  years,  it's   that  they  are  the  best  in  the  business.   And  while  none  of  us  at  the  Coin  Bureau   are  coding  wizards,  we  bet  that  a  fix   for  the  poisoning  problem  could  be  out   there  somewhere.  That  said,  today's   report  has  certainly  highlighted  some   fundamental  problems  Ethereum  needs  to   address  and  quickly.  If  left  unresolved,   Ethereum  could  face  serious  issues  and   even  be  pushed  down  the  market  cap   rankings  by  competitors.  But  fingers   crossed,  it  never  comes  to  that.  Okay,   folks,  that's  just  about  enough  from  me.   And  now  it's  time  to  hear  from  you.  Are   you  worried  about  Ethereum's  future,  or   are  you  planning  to  stack  ETH  at  a   discount  in  the  bare  market?  Let  us  know   in  the  comments  below.  If  this  video  has   you  wondering  what  happens  if  ETH  goes   to  zero,  then  check  out  the  video  over   here.  And  if  you  want  to  know  more  about   why  Vitalik  has  been  dumping  ETH,  well,   you  can  find  out  in  this  video  right   over  here.  Okay,  thank  you  all  for   watching  and  I'll  see  you  again  soon.   This  is  Guy  signing  off.
summary_hu: A videóban bemutatott elemzés szerint az Ethereum Fusaka frissítése jelentős problémákat okozott a protokoll gazdasági modelljében. A frissítés következtében csökkentek a tranzakciós díjak, ami gyengítette a díjak égetési mechanizmusát, és így a validátorok kevesebb jutalmat kapnak. A kutatás azt is megállapította, hogy a poisoning tranzakciók, amelyek célzott támadásként működnek, a frissítés óta jelentősen megnőtt, és már a tranzakciók egy jelentős hányadát teszik ki. A Bitmine vezérigazgatójának optimizmusa szemben áll a jelentésben leírt bearish előrejelzésekkel, amelyek szerint az Ethereum jövője komoly kihívásokkal néz szembe. Az elemzők figyelmeztetnek, hogy ha a fejlesztők nem kezelik ezeket a problémákat, az Ethereum a piaci versenytársak szorításába kerülhet.
summary_en: The analysis presented indicates that Ethereum's Fusaka upgrade has caused significant issues within the protocol's economic model. Following the upgrade, transaction fees have decreased, weakening the fee burn mechanism and resulting in lower rewards for validators. The report highlights a dramatic increase in poisoning transactions, which serve as targeted attacks and now constitute a substantial portion of all transactions. The bullish stance of Bitmine's chairman contrasts with the bearish predictions outlined in the report, suggesting that Ethereum faces serious challenges ahead. Analysts warn that if developers do not address these problems, Ethereum could be pressured by competing chains.
key_points_hu: A Fusaka frissítés csökkentette a tranzakciós díjakat, ami gyengítette a gazdasági modellt.
A poisoning tranzakciók aránya drámaian megnőtt, már a tranzakciók jelentős részét képezik.
Bitmine vezérigazgatójának optimizmusa nem áll összhangban a jelentés pesszimista előrejelzéseivel.
key_points_en: The Fusaka upgrade decreased transaction fees, weakening the economic model.
The proportion of poisoning transactions has dramatically increased, constituting a significant part of all transactions.
The optimism of Bitmine's chairman is at odds with the report's pessimistic forecasts.
